DashboardNext Lesson

Using useState

react.gg
Using useState

Previously, you learned that useState is a hook that allows you to persist a value across renders and trigger a re-render when the value changes.

You also learned how to use it. Specifically that useState returns an array with two values, the first being the current value of the state, and the second being a function that can be used to update the state.

Though creating a simple Counter app is cool and gives us a gentle introduction to useState, it's not super practical. Let's look at some other use cases that'll give us more insight into how we can leverage useState in more scenarios.

Specifically, we'll have an app that walks through the following state progression.

  1. 1. We'll start with a single piece of state
  2. 2. We'll add multiple pieces of state to the component
  3. 3. We'll update our state to see how we can manage state when it's an object
  4. 4. We'll update our state to see how we can manage state when it's an array of objects

Naturally, though it's a little stereotypical, the best app to build to see this progression in isolation is a Todo app.

🫵

Don't be fooled

Don't let the simple nature of this app fool you. After this lesson, you'll have a first principles understanding for managing state in React, and you'll be able to apply it to any app you build – regardless of the complexity.

In fact, if you've felt that the course has been too simple so far, this is the lesson where it starts to ramp up.

First, let's start with the UI for a single todo item. Eventually we'll create these dynamically whenever a user adds a new todo item to their list, but for now we'll hard code it.

Next we need to make our app interactive. Specifically, we want to give the user the ability to toggle the status of the todo item.

To do that, we'll add some state to the component to keep track of the status of the todo item, add an event handler to update that state, then hook the event handler up to our input via its onChange prop.

At this point, whenever the user clicks on the checkbox, completed will be set to the opposite of whatever it was and the component will re-render with the new value.

Cool, and mostly worthless - so far. Let's add some more functionality.

Specifically, let's add the ability for the user to edit our single todo item.

First, to make our todo item editable, we need to move it out of our JSX and into a state variable so it will persist across renders.

Next, we'll need some state to keep track of whether the user is currently editing the todo item.

And then we'll add a method that toggles that new editing state and hook that method up to a button the user can click.

Now, because we have our editing state, we can dynamically show/hide our input field based on that state.

At this point whenever the user clicks on our Edit button, the todo item will be replaced with an input element.

Now we need to make sure that whenever the user updates this input field, we also update the label on our state.

To do that, we'll create a new handleUpdateLabel method and pass that as an onChange prop to our input field. Now whenever the user edits the value in the input field, we'll update our label state.

At this point our app is progressing nicely, but you may notice something a little strange. Currently, we have multiple pieces of state representing one todo item. That works, but it won't scale well when we add more todo items.

How can we fix this?

What if we represented our todo item as an object instead of individual state values?

Now we need to make two changes to our code to match our new state structure.

First, we'll update our JSX to reference label and completed as properties on our todo state rather than as individual variables.

And then we'll update our event handlers to handle our todo state as an object rather than as individual pieces of state.

Todo.jsx
import * as React from "react"

export default function Todo () {
const [todo, setTodo] = React.useState({
id: 1,
label: "Learn React",
completed: false,
})
const [completed, setCompleted] = React.useState(false)
const [editing, setEditing] = React.useState(false)

const handleCheckboxClick = () => setTodo({
...todo,
completed: !todo.completed
})

const handleEditClick = () => setEditing(!editing)

const handleUpdateLabel = (e) => setTodo({
...todo,
label: e.target.value
})

return (
<div>
<label htmlFor="checkbox">
<div>
<input
type="checkbox"
id="checkbox"
checked={todo.completed}
onChange={handleCheckboxClick}
/>
<span />
</div>
{editing === true ? (
<input
type="text"
value={todo.label}
onChange={handleUpdateLabel}
/>
) : (
<span>{todo.label}</span>
)}
</label>
<button onClick={handleEditClick}>
{editing ? "Save" : "Edit"}
</button>
</div>
)
}

Updating Object State

It's important to note that when you pass a value to useState's updater function, whatever value you pass will always replace the current piece of state.

What this means is that if you have a piece of state that is an object, it won't be merged with the current state.

const [user, setUser] = React.useState({
id: 1,
name: "Tyler McGinnis",
email: "tyler@ux.dev"
})

const handleUpdateEmailIncorrectly = (email) => {
/*
This will overwrite our user state
with { email }.
*/
setUser({ email })
}

Instead of replacing the object, you most likely want to merge the new value with the existing state object. To do that, we can use JavaScript's spread operator to spread all of the existing key/value pairs onto a new object, then pass that new object to the updater function.

const handleUpdateEmail = (email) => {
const updatedUser = {
...user,
email: "tyler@ui.dev"
}

setUser(updatedUser) // 🫡
}

At this point our app is progressing slowly nicely. Unfortunately, it still doesn't resemble much of a todo list.

Instead of just rendering a single Todo element as we're doing currently, we want to render a Todo element for each todo item the user creates. This highlights a problem with how we've currently architected our app.

Right now the state of each todo item is coupled to our Todo component.

export default function Todo () {
const [todo, setTodo] = React.useState({
id: 1,
label: "Learn React",
completed: false,
})

...
}

On the surface, this seems like a good idea. We're keeping the state of each todo item close to the component that renders it. The problem is it's too rigid.

With this architecture, how would we go about adding another todo item? Your intuition might be to just render another Todo element.

export default function TodoList () {
return (
<ul>
<Todo />
<Todo />
</ul>
)
}

But now we have multiple todo items with the same label and id, and not only that, but what happens when we want the user to be able to create their own todo items?

export default function TodoList () {
return (
<ul>
<TodoComposer />
<Todo />
<Todo />
</ul>
)
}

How do we make it so our TodoComposer element can dynamically create new Todo elements?

What if instead of having each Todo component manage its own state, we lift that state up to the parent TodoList component?

This would solve all of our problems. TodoList would own (and manage) the state of each todo item and then we can pass data down to components that need it via props.

🏋️‍♀️

Lifting State Up

Learning when and how to lift state up is one of the most important aspects of state management in React.

Here's the rule of thumb – whenever you have state that multiple components depend on, you'll want to lift that state up to the nearest parent component and then pass it down via props.

The only gotcha is how you update that state. Often times when lifting state up, you decouple where the state lives from the event handlers that update that state.

To solve this, you'll create an updater function in the parent component where the state lives and, via props, invoke that function from the child component(s) where the event handler(s) live.

So first, let's lift our todo state up to a new TodoList component and then pass it down via a prop to the Todo component.

And now, before we update our Todo component, we need a way, from inside of that Todo component, to update the state that's now located in this parent TodoList component.

Whenever you're in a situation like this, what you'll want to do is create a function in the component where the state is located, and then pass that function down via props.

Then, you can pass data back up to the parent component by passing an argument to the function you passed down.

Now back inside our Todo component, we need to move some things around.

First, we'll remove the local todo state and accept it as a prop since it now lives inside of the parent TodoList component.

Next, since we'll now be rendering multiple Todo elements, we'll update the htmlFor and the input's id to be unique based on the todo's id.

Next, we'll update our event handlers to use our new handleUpdateTodo prop that we also passed down from the parent TodoList component.

The biggest thing to notice is the relationship between handleUpdateTodo in the parent component, and handleCheckboxClick and handleEditTodo in the child component.

I'll reiterate because it's perhaps the most important aspect of this lesson.

Whenever the state you're updating lives in a different location from the event handlers that update that state, you'll create an updater function in the component where the state lives and you'll invoke that function from the component where the event handlers live.

In our case, the state of our app lives inside of the parent TodoList component. Therefore, any time we want to update that state, we need to do so where it lives – in TodoList.

Notice that handleUpdateTodo has an updatedTodo parameter. This is how we get data from where the event handlers live, in a child component, back up to where the state lives, in the parent component.

It doesn't matter where handleUpdateTodo is called, it just matters that when it's called, it receives this updatedTodo argument.

So now inside of the child Todo component, we need to make sure when we invoke handleUpdateTodo, we pass it the updated todo item.

At this point our app is in better shape than it was before, but it's still not a todo list. Let's fix that.

In JavaScript, the way you can create a list of items is by using an array.

We'll do the same thing here by updating the state in our TodoList component to be an array of todo objects, rather than just a single todo object.

Now if you try to edit a todo item, you'll notice that we get an error. That's because we need to update our handleUpdateTodo function to treat todos as an array, rather than a single todo object.

Before we can do that, let's take a moment to learn about how you can update arrays that are on state.

TodoList.jsx
Todo.jsx
import * as React from "react"
import Todo from "./Todo"

export default function TodoList() {
const [todos, setTodos] = React.useState([
{ id: 1, label: "Learn React", completed: false },
{ id: 2, label: "Learn Next.js", completed: false },
{ id: 3, label: "Learn React Query", completed: false }
])

const handleUpdateTodo = (updatedTodo) => {
setTodos(updatedTodo)
}

return (
<ul>
{todos.map((todo) => (
<Todo
key={todo.id}
todo={todo}
handleUpdateTodo={handleUpdateTodo}
/>
))}
</ul>
)
}

Updating Array State

Similar to objects, whenever you want to update an array that's on state, you need to pass the updater function a new array which will replace the current array.

Admittedly, the knowledge you need for updating array state in React doesn't have anything to actually do with React, it's all just JavaScript.

This is a good thing because it means this knowledge is portable to other JavaScript projects, not just React specific ones.

You can break the process of updating arrays down into three use cases: adding elements, removing elements, and updating elements.

Here's the canonical way to do each.

To add an element to an array, use JavaScript's spread operator (...) to spread all the existing elements onto a new array with the new element.

const handleNewHighScore = (session) => {
const newHighScores = [...highScores, session]
setHighScores(newHighScores)
}

To remove an element from an array, use JavaScript's filter method to create a new array, filtering out the element that should be removed.

const handleRemoveCheater = (id) => {
const newHighScores = highScores.filter((session) =>
session.id !== id
)
setHighScores(newHighScores)
}

To update an element, use JavaScript's map method to create a new array, updating the specific element where appropriate.

const handleUpdateHighScore = (updatedSession) => {
const newHighScores = highScores.map((session) => {
return session.id === updatedSession.id
? updatedSession
: session
})
setHighScores(newHighScores)
}

The take away from all of these examples is we're never modifying the original state array, and we're using built-in JavaScript array methods to make our transformations.

Regardless of what type of changes you're making, these things should always hold true.

With this knowledge, we can now update our handleUpdateTodo function to treat todos as an array.

We'll use .map to create a new array, updating the specific todo item that was passed to handleUpdateTodo.

One thing to notice is that we didn't have to change the implementation of Todo.

All Todo cares about is it receives a handleUpdateTodo prop that it can invoke, passing it the updated todo item. What happens when it's invoked is an implementation detail of TodoList and is irrelevant to Todo.

At this point things are progressing nicely. Before we worry about adding new todos, let's give the user the ability to delete them.

Again, the rule of thumb is we want to update the state where it lives.

In this case, because TodoList is the component that owns our todos state, we'll want to create our function for deleting todo items in the same component.

Let's call it handleDeleteTodo and have it take in the id of the todo item to delete as its only argument.

From there, we'll pass handleDeleteTodo down as a prop to our Todo component.

And finally, we'll add our new handleDeleteTodo prop to an event handler inside of Todo, and then invoke that event handler whenever the user clicks on our new delete button.

At this point, we have the world's most useless todo app. Though peaceful, without the ability to add new todos, it's not very useful.

Let's start with changes to our TodoList component.

One more time for those in the back, because TodoList is the component that owns our todos state, we'll want to create our function for adding todo items in the same component.

Let's call it handleAddTodo and have it take in a new todo item which it'll add to our todos state.

Now we need to figure out where to invoke handleAddTodo. We could invoke it in TodoList, but that would require us to add a new input field and state to our TodoList component.

Instead, let's abstract that into another component, we'll call it TodoComposer and it'll receive our handleAddTodo function as a prop.

Now inside of TodoComposer, all we need to do is get the new todo item from the user, and then invoke props.handleAddTodo with it.

First, we'll make out TodoComposer component.

Now let's add some state and an input field to our component to keep track of the label of the new todo item.

Next, let's create a function that will handle actually creating our new todo object.

This function would usually live on a server somewhere (to generate unique ids), but for our use case we'll just fake it.

From there we'll create an event handler that will invoke the handleAddTodo prop, passing it our newly created todo item and then clear the state of the input field.

And finally, we need to hook our handleAddTodoClick event handler up to a new button the user can click to add their new todo item.

And that's it! We now have a fully functioning todo app.

TodoList.jsx
Todo.jsx
TodoComposer.jsx
import * as React from "react"

function createTodo (label) {
return {
id: Math.floor(Math.random() * 10000),
label,
completed: false,
}
}

export default function TodoComposer({ handleAddTodo }) {
const [label, setLabel] = React.useState("")

const handleUpdateLabel = (e) => setLabel(e.target.value)

const handleAddTodoClick = () => {
const todo = createTodo(label)
handleAddTodo(todo)
setLabel("")
}

return (
<li>
<input
placeholder="Add a new todo"
type="text"
value={label}
onChange={handleUpdateLabel}
/>
<button
disabled={label.length === 0}
onClick={handleAddTodoClick}
>
Add Todo
</button>
</li>
)
}

Though it may have been... painstaking, hopefully at this point you feel confident with all aspects of managing state in React – including updating objects/array state as well as lifting state up.
